question: Read the following poem and write an appreciation of it with the help of the given points in paragraph format. Night of the Scorpion I remember the night my mother was stung by a scorpion.
answer:
1Show SolutionAppreciation of the poem ‘The Night of the Scorpion.’
The poem ‘The Night of the Scorpion’ by Nissim Ezekiel revolves around the sting of a scorpion that the poet’s mother experienced on a rainy night. The poem follows a narrative style, with an incident recounted in free verse with no fixed rhyme scheme. The poem is enriched with various figures of speech, such as Alliteration, Antithesis, Hyperbole, Inversion, Metaphor, Onomatopoeia, Oxymoron, Repetition, Simile, and transferred Epithet. An example of personification from the poem is ‘I watched the flame feeding on my mother’ as the flame is given the human quality of ‘feeding’. The poem is a first-person account of how a son(the poet) watches helplessly as his mother suffers from a scorpion’s sting. The villagers’ blessings and curses, a holy man’s chants and the rational husband’s experiments cannot bring relief to the poet’s mother. It shows how the behaviour of the villages is characterised by their illiteracy and lack of medical facilities, which result in blind beliefs and superstitions. Nissim Ezekiel's ‘Night of the Scorpion’ is a strong yet simple statement on the power of self-effacing love. The ironic twist in the poem comes when, in the end, the mother who suffered in silence opens her mouth and says ‘Thank God the scorpion picked on me and spared my children.’ Yes, I like this poem because it mentions the bond between the mother and son.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 3.1 - Night of the Scorpion

question: Prepare a Presentation (on paper or on a PC) as a piece of reference to other students. Make use of the following points. (1) Title page (2) Introduction of the poet
answer:
Title: 
Night of the Scorpion
Introduction of the poet: 
Nissim Ezekiel (1924-2004) was an Indian Jewish poet, actor, playwright, editor, and art critic. He was a foundational figure in postcolonial India’s literary history, particularly in the wider range of Indian literature. The poem depicts the selfless love of a mother who is stung by a scorpion.
The complete poem:
Night of the Scorpion
I remember the night my mother
was stung by a scorpion. Ten hours
of steady rain had driven him
to crawl beneath a sack of rice.
Parting with his poison – flash
of diabolic tail in the darkroom –
 he risked the rain again
The peasants came like swarms of flies
and buzzed the name of God a hundred times
to paralyze the Evil One.
With candles and with lanterns
throwing giant scorpion shadows
on the mud-baked walls
they searched for him: he was not found.
They clicked their tongues.
With every movement that the scorpion made his poison moved in Mother’s blood, they said.
May he sit still, they said
May the sins of your previous birth be burned away tonight, they said.
May your suffering decrease the misfortunes of your next birth, they said.
May the sum of all evil balanced in this unreal world against the sum of good become diminished by your pain.
May the poison purify your flesh of desire, and your spirit of ambition,
they said, and they sat around on the floor with my mother in the center,
the peace of understanding on each face. 
More candles, more lanterns, more neighbors,
more insects, and the endless rain.
My mother twisted through and through,
groaning on a mat.
My father, sceptic, rationalist,
trying every curse and blessing,
powder, mixture, herb, and hybrid
He even poured a little paraffin
upon the bitten toe and put a match to it. 
I watched the flame feeding on my mother.
I watched the holy man perform his rites to tame the poison with an incantation.
After twenty hours it lost its sting.
My mother only said
Thank God the scorpion picked on me
And spared my children.
-Nissim Ezekiel
Learning objectives: 
My mother only said
Thank God the scorpion picked on me
And spared my children.
I watched the flame feeding on my mother.
I watched the holy man perform his rites to tame the poison with an incantation.
Style of writing: 
(This is a narrative poem in the first person, lines of irregular length, free verse, absence of rhyme and meter, enjambement. Style is free and colloquial. There is repeated use of indirect speech, Imagery, use of similes, metaphors, alliteration, repetition, and onomatopoeia.)
Scorpion lines:
I remember the night my mother was stung by a scorpion. Ten hours of steady rain had driven him to crawl beneath a sack of rice. Parting with his poison - a flash of diabolic tail in the darkroom - he risked the rain again.
The peasants:
The peasants came like swarms of flies
and buzzed the name of God a hundred times
to paralyze the Evil One.
The poet’s father:
 (sceptic, rationalist. anxious, worried)
The poet’s mother: 
(brave, patient, selfless, loving, understanding)
Vocabulary: 
The poem is simple and easy to understand. The poem is enriched with various figures of speech, such as Alliteration, Antithesis, Hyperbole, Inversion, Metaphor, Onomatopoeia, Oxymoron, Repetition, Simile, and transferred Epithet. An example of personification from the poem is “I watched the flame feeding on my mother” as the flame is given the human quality of ‘feeding’. 
Credits: 
The ironic twist in the poem comes when, in the end, the mother who suffered in silence opens her mouth. She says, ‘Thank God the scorpion picked on me and spared my children.’ Yes, I like this poem because it mentions the bond between the mother and son.
subject: English

question: ‘Music is the medicine of the mind’. Narrate an experience that leads to the above conclusion. Write it in your own words, in your notebook.
answer:
‘Music is the medicine of the mind.’
I was appearing for my class IX final examinations last year when this incident occurred. A few months earlier, we had moved to Thane, and I had to commute by train to and from my school. My final exams were scheduled at 7 a.m, I took the usual 6:12 train from Thane, and just as the train left the station, it stopped abruptly. Ten minutes later, everybody in the train compartment started panicking. I calculated that if the train did not start in another five minutes, I would be late for my exam. That’s when I decided to listen to the light sitar music that my mother had downloaded on my phone. My mind slowly began to calm down, and though I arrived at the exam hall a good 15 minutes late, I did well and completed my paper, all because the music helped me relax before the examination. Music is indeed a medicine for the mind.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 3.2 - The Night I Met Einstein

question: You will come across many famous personalities in the story. Go to library or search on internet the following name and write a few lines about the personality given below. Albert Einstein

From an early age, he was fascinated by the laws of nature and felt a deep sense of wonder at the invisible force that directed a compass needle. He is best known for his Special and General Theories of Relativity and for the concept of mass-energy equivalence, expressed by the famous equation E = mc2.

question: You will come across many famous personalities in the story. Go to library or search on internet the following name and write a few lines about the personality given below. Johann S. Bach
answer: Born on 21st March, 1685, this German came from a family of musicians. His father, Johann Ambrosius, worked as a town musician in Eisenach, Germany and taught young Johann to play the violin. Bach had a beautiful soprano voice, which helped him secure a place at a school in Lüneburg. Regarded as perhaps the greatest composer of all time, Bach was known during his lifetime primarily as an outstanding organ player and technician.

question: Make a list of Hawking’s physical inabilities after 1963.
answer:
Following is the list of physical inabilities that Hawking faced after 1963:

Physical control of Hawking’s body decreased.
The disease started slowing down his abilities.

question: Explain how the life of Stephen Hawking proves the proverb ‘Every cloud has a silver lining’. Write it down in your notebook.
answer:
Every cloud has a silver lining.
 This proverb 'Every cloud has a silver lining means that you should never feel hopeless because difficult times always lead to better days. Difficult times are like dark clouds that pass overhead and block the sun. But at the same time, on the outer edge of the clouds, you will invariably observe a silver lining that provides some measure of light and hope.
 The problems and difficulties that we face in life are like those dark clouds. Stephen Hawking began experiencing health problems when he was barely 21, a student at Oxford. On occasion, he would trip and fall or slur his speech. Later on, he was diagnosed with ALS, Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis. It was a disease in which the nerves that controlled his muscles were shutting down. He was given two and a half years to live. But as we know, he went on to live more than a half-century beyond.
 Thanks to early diagnosis, advanced medical treatment, and complete support from family, friends, and students, he lived a full life and made major contributions to existing theories in physics and cosmology. These, in fact, were the silver linings that brightened what would otherwise have been a lacklustre life.
 The life of Stephen Hawking proves that even though the difficulties we face might seem enormous, there is always hope, there are always ways to circumvent them and to keep on achieving and contributing to the world.
subject: English

question: You have come to know from the text that ‘The Theory of Everything’ is a film describing the life and work of Stephen Hawking. Watch the film on internet and write a complete review of it.
answer:
Title: The Theory of Everything refers to both Stephen Hawking’s scientific quest for a unifying equation of the universe and the expansive power of human resilience and love.
Characters: Eddie Redmayne delivers an Oscar-winning, physically transformative performance as Stephen Hawking. Felicity Jones brings immense depth and quiet strength to the role of his devoted wife, Jane.
Story: Based on Jane Hawking’s memoir, the film tracks Stephen’s life from his vibrant student days at Cambridge, his devastating ALS diagnosis at age 21, his rise to scientific fame, and the emotional toll the illness takes on his marriage.
Dialogues: The dialogues blend complex physics with deeply emotional human interactions. Stephen’s famous line, “Where there is life, there is hope,” beautifully encapsulates the script’s philosophical depth.
Picturisation: Directed by James Marsh, the film uses warm, golden tones for early life and tight, intimate framing later on to show that while Stephen’s body becomes confined, his mind remains infinitely vast.
Music: The moving, piano-and-string heavy score by Jóhann Jóhannsson perfectly mirrors the film’s shifts between scientific triumph and personal heartbreak.
Special Features: The exceptional physical authenticity of Redmayne’s performance led Stephen Hawking himself to allow the filmmakers to use his actual copyrighted computer-synthesised voice.
Opinion/Views: It is an exceptionally moving, honest biopic. It succeeds by focusing not just on the science but on the raw realities of love, sacrifice, and human vulnerability.
Message/Moral Values: The ultimate message is that physical limitations cannot chain a determined mind. It highlights the profound resilience of the human spirit and the immense value of caregivers.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 3.3 - Stephen Hawking

question: Work in group and prepare and present a speech on ‘How to Achieve Success.’ (You may take help of the poem).
answer:
How to achieve Success
Good afternoon, dear friends! I, Ms. Priya Desai, am here to speak to you about ‘How to achieve success’.
Success is the sweet fruit of sustained effort, earned after putting one’s best into achieving a goal. The quest for success is a tough one, ridden with much dejection. It is rare to find a person who has achieved success with just a little hard work. It is said that as an inventor, Edison made 1000 unsuccessful attempts before he successfully made a Consistent Light Bulb. It is believed that KFC’s Colonel Sanders’ secret recipe for the Kentucky Fried Chicken was rejected 1009 times before someone accepted it. The rest is history.
Thus, there are no short-cuts to success. One has to take the stairs, where every step is rife with hard work, persistence, determination, a never-say-die attitude, and tons of sacrifice.
So then, my dear friends, all you need in life is the will to overcome the fear of failure and the confidence to move ahead. Success will surely follow. Thank you.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 3.4 - The Will to Win

question: Read the following poem and write an appreciation of the poem ‘The Will to Win’ in a paragraph format. The Will to Win If you want a thing bad enough To go out and fight for it, Work day and night
answer:
1Show Solution
Berton Braley has written this inspirational poem, The Will To Win'.
The poem has no specific rhyme scheme, but rhymes are used randomly all throughout. The most common type is lines ending with the word ‘it’; e.g., ‘for it’, ‘of it’, ‘of it’; ‘without it’, ‘about it’; ‘beget it’, ‘get it'; 'sweat for if, ‘fret for if, and so on. Other examples are ‘capacity', 'sagacity’, ‘tenacity’: ‘pain’, ‘brain’.
The main figure of speech used is repetition, as seen in the abundance of ‘for if phrases used throughout the poem. The other figures of speech are Climax, Tautology, Antithesis, etc.
The poem is rich in ideas for personality traits and circumstances that aid or hamper success.
My favourite line is If dogged and grim you. This line is a very forceful Alliteration, motivating us to persist forcefully with our efforts in order to achieve success.
Special Features

The poem is motivational and inspirational, encouraging readers to work hard and never give up.
The language is simple yet powerful, making it easy to understand and relate to.
The tone of the poem is encouraging and determined, pushing the reader to strive for success.

I like this poem because it encourages me to be persistent and to keep on striving for success - because that's the only way to achieve it. This is an inspirational poem. It motivates one to set targets and achieve goals.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 3.4 - The Will to Win

question: What prime quality did Mary Kom display during her first attempt in Olympic games? What was her bad luck?
answer: During her first attempt in the Olympic Games, Mary Kom displayed the prime quality of resilience and determination. Despite facing challenges and setbacks, she showcased an unwavering commitment to her sport and a never-give-up attitude. However, her bad luck was evident when the judges did not favour her during the sparring session with her counter-puncher, Adams. This unfortunate turn of events, in which decisions did not go in her favour, highlighted the subjective nature of judging in sports competitions and its impact on an athlete's performance and results.

question: Write a short paragraph describing the life and work of Mary Kom. Take the help of the points given below. You can take the help of the fact file that you have prepared earlier.
answer:
The Life and Work of Mary Kom
Mary Kom, the legendary Indian boxer, is an inspiring example of determination and hard work. Born into a poor family of tenant farmers in Manipur, she faced financial hardships early in life, but her passion for sports never faded. Inspired by the achievements of boxer Dingko Singh, she overcame societal opposition to pursue boxing. Her rigorous training began under Coach K. Kosana Meitei, where she spent hours mastering her technique with immense dedication. This hard work led to historic achievements: Mary Kom became a record six-time World Amateur Boxing Champion and won a Bronze Medal at the 2012 London Olympics, earning the nickname “Magnificent Mary.” Looking ahead, she is dedicated to running her boxing academy in Manipur to train young, talented athletes from humble backgrounds, helping them fulfil their dreams of winning Olympic glory for India.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 3.5 - Unbeatable Super Mom-Mary Kom

question: ‘What men can do, women can do better.’ Divide your class in 2 groups. Let one group offer points ‘For the topic’ and the other, ‘Against the topic.’ Note down the points in your notebooks and expand
answer:
View: Men cannot compare to women
Women have inherent qualities not possessed by men that enable them to do things better than men. There was a time when certain professions or careers were considered the sole domain of men. In recent years, women have pooh-poohed the very idea.
Women have more patience and endurance than men. Hence, when entrusted with any task, they stick it out and see it through. They are painstaking and focus on every detail. They can see the overall picture, unlike men who prioritise short-term benefits and instant solutions.
Women tend to communicate more effectively than men, focusing on creating solutions that work for the group, talking through issues, and utilising nonverbal cues such as tone, emotion, and empathy, whereas men tend to be more task-oriented, less talkative, and more isolated. Men have a more difficult time understanding emotions that are not explicitly verbalised, while women tend to intuit emotions and emotional cues.
Men tend to have a ‘fight or flight’ response to stressful situations, while women seem to approach these situations with a tend or befriend' strategy.
Hence, women do very well as PR Managers, HR Managers, and Psychologists. Psychiatrists, etc., which were previously considered exclusive, male-dominated fields of work.
Counterview: Men are the superior species
With no disrespect or offence intended. When it comes to the job front, men are superior to women. They have the discipline, the psychological assurance and presence, and most importantly. the time to sit over time and meet targets.
Women tend to be straightforward, outspoken, and curt in their dealings with clients. Men are more polite, patient, and considerate, especially with irate customers. This is because they are more hormonally stable and balanced than women. They tend to view things from a long-term viewpoint and are focused on the goals of the establishment they work for
Men tend to process better in the left hemisphere of the brain, while women tend to process equally well in both hemispheres. This difference explains why men are generally stronger with left-brain activities and approach problem-solving from a task-oriented perspective.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 3.5 - Unbeatable Super Mom-Mary Kom

question: Two famous proverbs - ‘Actions speak louder than words’ and ‘Fortune favours the brave’ are best suited to the personality of Mary Kom. Form two groups in the class. Each group should write
answer:
ctions Speak Louder Than Words
Mary Kom’s life and personality exemplify the proverb ‘Actions speak louder than words’. Right from an early age, she was interested in athletics and boxing. Her father, a former wrestler, opposed her entering boxing for fear of disfigurement, but Mary Kom went ahead, trained rigorously, left her home state, and pursued her goal.
There are many who express desires and ambitions, but limit themselves to highfalutin talk. Mary Kom made her dreams a reality and went on to win a medal at the Olympics for India. Her words always echoed humility. She avoided blaming others for her losses and took responsibility for them herself.
Mary Kom’s life has been a journey of high-voltage action, despite being, by nature, a shy, soft-spoken, gentle girl.
(2) Fortune Favours The Brave
It is not easy for a girl to enter and participate in a male-dominated field such as boxing. Mary Kom hailed from Manipur, which is a conservative state, to say the least.
Mary Kom, however. was brave enough to challenge gender bias, opposition from her father, and the rigours of sparring with professional male boxers in Pune.
Her daring and courage were rewarded with awards and national recognition. But the icing on the cake was when she went on to win international events. finally culminating in winning the bronze medal at the 2012 Olympic event.
Lady Luck had smiled on her for her persistence, continual effort, and unwavering focus on her goals. Indeed, Mary Kom has proved through her life and achievements that if one has the spirit of challenging the odds, one will surely reap rich dividends.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 3.5 - Unbeatable Super Mom-Mary Kom

question: Look at the web diagram given below and study the traits of a good concert organizer. 10 Essential qualities of a concert organizer Professionalism Patience Respectfulness An open mind
answer:
Professionalism: behaves respectfully, follows through on promises, and manages artists/audience with proper etiquette.
Patience: stays calm under pressure (delays, last‑minute changes) and handles problems without panic.
Respectfulness: treats artists, crew, volunteers and audience fairly and courteously.
An open mind: welcomes new ideas, adapts plans, and listens to feedback.
Big‑picture view: plans overall flow (schedule, safety, budget) while keeping event goals in mind.
Ability to entrust responsibility to the right person: delegates tasks to competent people and trusts them to deliver.
Strong and effective communication: gives clear instructions to performers, crew, venue and publicity teams.
Decisiveness: makes timely decisions when faced with choices or crises.
Self‑awareness: knows own strengths/limits, asks for help, and learns from mistakes.
Motivator: inspires and encourages the team to do their best and stay committed.
subject: English

question: The whole story revolves around Anant. Write a short paragraph on Anant. Take help of the following points to develop the paragraph. 1. Health 2. Disease 3. Early interests 4. Intense desire/wish
answer: nant was a 15-year-old. a schoolboy who was exceptionally talented and ambitious. He was the best table tennis player and the fastest runner in his school. A passionate musician, Anant was also learning to play the sitar and astonished everyone, including his guru, when he began composing his own tunes at an early stage of his training. When everything seemed to be just right for him, he was unfortunate. Unfortunately, he was diagnosed with cancer, and the deadly disease was ruthlessly threatening to cut short his promising life abruptly. He was, however, determined to win the battle against the disease and start life afresh. As a music enthusiast and a sitar learner, he had a keen desire. to attend Pandit Ravi Shankar’s concert. Anant’s loving family tried to fulfil all his wishes since they knew that his time with them was limited. This wish, however, was beyond their reach as Anant was physically too weak to move. This saddened them deeply. As a bold solution to their problem, his sister Smita arranged for the dream concert at their Aunt’s residence itself, and Anant was overjoyed to finally witness his dream in reality.

question: The two contradictory pictures are depicted in the story. Discuss in pairs and describe them in your words in front of the class.
answer:
This story presents two contradictory images - one of extreme excitement and jubilation, and the other, which is painful and heartbreaking.
The siblings’ excitement on hearing about Pandit Ravi Shankar’s performance at a nearby auditorium, Anant’s eagerness to be able to enjoy the performance, and Smita’s delight on witnessing the ‘Once in a lifetime’ concert depict happiness, joy, anticipation and hopefulness.
On the other hand, Smita’s efforts in fulfilling Anant’s desire to witness the concert, the manner with which she pleads to the maestros to perform for her brother and the mixed feelings evoked as Anant savours the concert in his Aunt’s house as life slowly leaves his body, bring tears to one’s eyes and leave us feeling deeply anguished.
subject: English

‘A’ Indian word
‘B’ Meaning

(a) A piece of Indian classical music based on one of the six basic musical modes.

2. Ustad
(b) An expert or highly skilled person, especially a musician.

3. Pandit
(c) A person with specialised knowledge or a teacher of any field of knowledge in Hinduism, particularly

4. Guru
(d) Hindus who have a guru strive to receive spiritual initiation from him.

5. Sitar
(e) A sitar is an Indian musical instrument with two layers of strings, a long neck, and a round body.

6. Tabla
(f) A pair of small hand drums used in Indian music, one of which is slightly larger than the other and is played using pressure from the heel of the hand to vary the pitch.

7. Sahib 
(g) Formerly a term of respect for important white Europeans in colonial India; used after the name

8. Mantra
(h) A sacred word or phrase repeated in meditation

9. Bazaar
(i) A market or group of shops in India.

question: The text mentions the names of stalwarts like Pandit Ravi Shankar and Ustad Allah Rakha. Both have earned worldwide name and fame for their outstanding contribution to the field of music.
answer:
Name: Michael Joseph Jackson
Field: Music
Specialisation: Singer, Songwriter and Dancer
Guru: His elder brother - Jackie, Tito, Jermaine, Marlon
Early life: Born in Gary, Indiana, on August 29, 1958. He was the 8th of ten children in his working-class African American family.
Education: He attended Montclair College Preparatory School.
Early success: In 1964, Michael and his brother Marlon joined the Jackson brothers, a band formed by their father and won a major local talent show. They would go on to become the world-famous Jackson 5, catapulting Michael to fame.
Contribution to the field and world: The concept of music videos as short films is one of his greatest accomplishments. He effectively broke the racial barrier, which proved a boon to several other African-American artists who followed him. He popularised the robot dance, breakdance, and many other dance styles, the latter two being almost exclusively associated with him.
Awards: Multiple Guinness World Records, including the Most Successful. Entertainer of All Time, 13 Grammy Awards, the Grammy Legend Award, the Grammy Lifetime Achievement Award, 24 American Music Awards, more than any other artist, including the “Artist of the Century”.
Any special incident that has occurred in his/her life: As a child, Michael’s mother would wake him up no matter what the time, to watch James Brown perform. James Brown was a major figure of popular music during that era, and Jackson was so mesmerised by him that he remained his biggest inspiration all his life.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 3.6 - The Concert

question: Imagine you are the monitor of your class. You are assigned the task to invite a classical singer as a chief guest for the Annual Day Programme.
answer:
M. K. Gandhi High School,
Palghar (East)
Mumbai - 400 123
23rd May, 2025
Shri. Suresh Wadkar
Saraswati Nivas,
Bandra (West),
Mumbai - 400 070
Subject: Invitation to preside as the Chief Guest for the Annual Day Programme in the school.
Respected Sir,
I, Master Pranit Joshi, attend M. K. Gandhi High School in Palghar. I’m writing on behalf of my school’s principal and teachers to invite you to serve as the Chief Guest for our school’s Annual Day Program.
Our school has been associated with music since its inception, as its founder, Mr Nityanand Jadhav, was a music connoisseur who encouraged children to learn various classical instruments. Each year on the Annual Day, our school invites stalwarts from different fields as the Chief Guest for the celebration to encourage and motivate the students. This year, since the programme’s theme is ‘Music’, we could not think of a better personality than you to grace the occasion.
All of us would be pleased if you accepted our invitation and agreed to be part of our happiness. We have attached a brochure along with the letter. It provides details of the programme. We would be highly obliged if you confirm your availability for the same.
Yours sincerely,
Mst. Pranit Joshi

question: Go through the poem again and write in your notebook an appreciation of the poem in the paragraph format.
answer:
ppreciation of the poem ‘A Thing of beauty is a Joy For Ever’
The poem, ‘A Thing of Beauty is a Joy For Ever’ by John Keats, is a part of the poem ‘Endymion’, which was composed just three years before he died at the young age of 25. The poem is written in rhyming couplets, and thus the rhyme scheme is ‘aabbcc'. The figures of speech used to enrich the poem include Alliteration, anticlimax, antithesis, assonance, climax, Consonance, Epigram, personification, and repetition. An example of Antithesis is ‘Such the sun, the moon, trees old and young,’ where contrasting ideas such as ‘old’ and ‘young’ are used in the same line to include all forms of living things on earth. The central idea conveyed through the poem is that the loveliness of the things of Beauty gives us unending pleasure, and they will never cease to exist. One can keep. relishing their sweetness irrespective of the limits of time and space. It depicts eternal hope because it frees objects of beauty from any expiry date. The best thing about beautiful things is that they help a person relax and give him/her a deep sense of satisfaction even in the worst of times.
subject: English

question: Go through the story again and find out various instances which create humour in ‘The Luncheon’. Complete the table by picking up various humorous instances and the particular line from the story.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.2 - The Luncheon

question: Who said these words/sentences? Under what circumstances? Words/Sentences 1. I never eat anything for luncheon. 2. It’s many years since we first met. 3. Are you still hungry?
answer:
Words/Sentences
Who said?
Under what circumstances?

1. I never eat anything for luncheon.
The woman
She said this early in the meal at Foyot’s to reassure the narrator and suggest moderation, despite later ordering multiple dishes (e.g., caviar, salmon).

2. It’s many years since we first met.
The woman
She remarked this during their reunion at the play, reflecting on the passage of time since their initial meeting and leading to the luncheon invitation.

3. Are you still hungry?
The narrator
He asked this faintly after the woman claimed one should leave a meal still able to eat more, reflecting his exhaustion and financial strain.

4. I don’t believe in overloading my stomach.
The woman
She said this while criticizing the narrator’s mutton chop, ironically, as she continued to eat caviar and salmon.

5. I’ll eat nothing for dinner tonight.
The narrator
He retorted this sarcastically as they parted, indicating his financial ruin after paying the exorbitant bill.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.2 - The Luncheon

question: Although the author was not a vindictive man, he was very happy to see her weigh twenty one stone and had finally had his revenge. What makes him say this? Explain.
answer: Twenty years earlier, the writer was earning barely enough money to make both ends meet. The lady wanted him to give her luncheon at Foyot’s, an expensive restaurant. The writer thought that he could stand her a modest luncheon. The lady reassured him by saying she had never eaten anything for luncheon. However, she ended by eating about six things, some of which were very expensive; yet she insisted till the end that she never ate more than one thing for luncheon and advised him against 'filling his stomach with a lot of meat' when all he had eaten was one small mutton chop. The writer was finally left with a whole month before him and no money in his pocket. He could not forget this incident, and when he met her twenty years later, she had become very fat, weighing 21 stone. This made the writer feel that though he was not a vindictive man, he had got his revenge.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.2 - The Luncheon

question: There are quite a few places where the author uses the expressions ‘My heart sank, panic seized’ etc. What was the reason for this? Explain.
answer: The author used these expressions to show his growing fear and anxiety about the increasing cost of the meal. He was poor and had limited money, and as the woman kept ordering expensive dishes like caviar, salmon, and asparagus, he worried about how he would pay the bill.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.2 - The Luncheon

question: What are the instances which create humour in “The Luncheon”?
answer: Humour arises from the woman’s hypocrisy (saying she eats only one thing but ordering many dishes), the narrator’s silent panic as the bill mounts, and his sarcastic remark at the end about not eating dinner. The contrast between her actions and words, along with his suffering, makes it funny.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.2 - The Luncheon

question: Describe the use of irony and humour in “The Luncheon”.
answer: When the woman claims she doesn’t eat much but orders pricey things, leaving the narrator bankrupt, irony is employed. Her ignorance of his struggle and his clever retaliation when he observes her weight gain years later are the sources of humour. When combined, they playfully draw attention to human shortcomings.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.2 - The Luncheon

question: Study the different uses of ‘could’. Identify what it indicates. Lack of rain could cause draught. (possibility/condition)
answer:
possibility
Explanation:
In this context, “could” expresses the likelihood or chance of an event occurring, suggesting that a lack of water is a realistic, potential result of insufficient rain rather than an absolute certainty. It is not acting as a “condition” here because conditions typically rely on an “if” clause (e.g., if it doesn’t rain, there will be a drought) to show that one specific action depends entirely on another.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.2 - The Luncheon

question: Study the different uses of ‘could’. Identify what it indicates. Abhi could perform well in his school days. (suggestion/past ability)
answer:
past ability
Explanation:
Here, ‘could’ serves as the past tense of ‘can,’ expressing that Abhi had the talent or capacity to achieve good results during a specific time that has already ended. It is not a suggestion because the sentence states a fact about his prior capabilities rather than offering advice for a current or future situation.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.2 - The Luncheon

question: Study the different uses of ‘could’. Identify what it indicates. Instead of playing computer games you could play real games with friends. (past ability/suggestion)
answer:
suggestion
Explanation:
Here, ‘could’ is used to offer an alternative idea or a gentle piece of advice for the present moment. It proposes a different, potentially better activity (playing real games) without sounding as forceful as ‘should’ or ‘must’. It is not a past ability because the sentence is talking about a choice available right now, rather than a skill the person used to have in the past.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.2 - The Luncheon

question: Study the different uses of ‘could’. Identify what it indicates. Could I use your computer for surfing net? (possibility/request)
answer:
request
Explanation:
Here, ‘could’ is used as a polite and respectful way to ask for permission to do something (in this case, to use someone else’s computer). It is not indicating a ‘possibility,’ because the speaker is actively asking the listener for a favour, rather than simply stating that an event might happen. Using ‘could’ instead of ‘can’ makes the request sound softer and more polite!
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.2 - The Luncheon

question: Study the different uses of ‘could’. Identify what it indicates. We could go on an excursion, if we didn’t have exam. (suggestion/condition)
answer:
condition
Explanation:
This sentence is a classic example of a conditional statement (specifically, the Second Conditional). The action of going on an excursion depends entirely on the hypothetical scenario established by the ‘if’ clause, without having an exam. Because the possibility of the trip is directly tied to a specific requirement being met, ‘could’ is used to express a conditional outcome rather than merely offer a general suggestion.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.2 - The Luncheon

question: On the occasion of Diwali, write a letter to your friend to invite him/her to celebrate the festival in an innovative way. Use the following hints. time and place other friends are also invited
answer:
951/NA,
PurpleValley,
Tiger Park,
Mumbai 400 028
2nd October, 2025
Dear Mayur,
I hope you are in good health and relaxing after the first-term examinations. I’m sure you must have done well as this is a crucial year for all of us. My exams have just ended too, and I am looking forward to some well-needed rest.
I am writing this letter so that we all get to unwind during the festival of Diwali, especially since we would all have to gear up for our Board exams after that. Hence, you are warmly invited to celebrate Diwali with my family on Sunday, 22nd October.
The Laxmi poojan will be followed by a special lunch along with Diwali goodies such as Mathri, Kaju Katli, and your favourite Gujia. Post-lunch, we have organised a fun programme and an antakshari competition, with the elders of the family teaming up against the younger members.
Prasad, Nandini, Anita and Shreya too would be joining us along with their respective families.
Please do come along with your family. I’m eagerly awaiting your arrival.
Yours lovingly,
Shreyas

chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.2 - The Luncheon

question: (a)“The Phantom Luncheon” by Saki. (b) “The Ant and the Grasshopper” by William Somerset Maugham. Form 4 groups of the class.
answer:
“The Phantom Luncheon” by Saki.

The Plot: A wealthy but stingy woman, Lady Gilneck, invites guests to an expensive restaurant. Her secret plan is to make them think it’s a casual “pay-for-yourself” lunch, so she can look like a grand hostess without paying a dime.
The Twist: One guest, Sir James, catches onto her cheapness. He deliberately orders the most luxurious items on the menu, and the other guests follow his lead. When the bill arrives, Sir James loudly thanks Lady Gilneck for her incredible generosity. To avoid social embarrassment, she is forced to pay the massive bill herself.
The Lesson: Greed and social hypocrisy will backfire when you try to trick the wrong people.

“The Ant and the Grasshopper” by William Somerset Maugham.

The Plot: This story flips Aesop’s famous fable on its head through two brothers. George is “the Ant hardworking, honest, and saving for the future. Tom is “the Grasshopper”, lazy, charming, and constantly borrowing money to live a life of luxury. George expects Tom to end up broke and miserable as punishment for his laziness.
The Twist: Instead of failing, Tom charms and marries a very wealthy, elderly woman. When she dies, she leaves him an absolute fortune. Tom becomes richer than George could ever dream of being.
The Lesson: Life isn’t always fair, and sometimes charm and luck beat hard work.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.2 - The Luncheon

question: Your teacher will explain the ‘Heritage Cycle’. à By understanding (cultural heritage) people value it. à By valuing it people want to care for it. à By caring for it, it will help people enjoy it.
answer:
The Complete Heritage Cycle:

From understanding comes valuing: When you learn the history and effort behind a monument, you begin to appreciate its worth.
From valuing comes caring: Once you realise how precious it is, you naturally want to protect it from damage or destruction.
From caring comes enjoyment: When a heritage site is well protected and cared for, it becomes a beautiful, welcoming place for people to visit and enjoy.
From enjoying comes a thirst to understand: When you have a wonderful experience at a heritage site, it sparks your curiosity to learn even more about it.
By understanding it: The cycle starts all over again, deeper and stronger than before!

question: Do you know the difference between: (a) World Heritage (b) Cultural Heritage Refer to a dictionary/encyclopedia/reference book/the internet to get the detailed meaning.
answer:
World Heritage
Cultural Heritage

This is a specific type of cultural or natural heritage considered of outstanding value to humanity.
This refers to the legacy of physical artefacts and intangible attributes of a society inherited from past generations.

It’s recognised and protected by UNESCO (United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organisation).
It includes tangible things like buildings, monuments, landscapes, artworks, and artefacts.

World Heritage sites can be cultural, natural, or a combination of both.
It also encompasses intangible elements like traditions, languages, folklore, and knowledge.

question: Write a report on any tourist place/historical place/World Heritage Site you recently visited using the points given below. (i) Title (ii) Place/Location (iii) Background/History
answer:
Educational Excursion to the Ajanta Caves
Recently, our Class visited the world-famous Ajanta Caves, a UNESCO World Heritage Site located in a horseshoe-shaped valley in the Chhatrapati Sambhajinagar district of Maharashtra. Dating from the 2nd century BCE to 480 CE, these thirty rock-cut Buddhist monuments served as ancient monasteries and prayer halls that were accidentally rediscovered by a British officer in 1819. The site is globally celebrated for its magnificent architecture, chiselled from solid basalt, and its stunning fresco paintings depicting the Jataka tales in natural mineral pigments. To preserve these delicate treasures, the Archaeological Survey of India enforces strict security policies, including mandatory bag scans, a strict ban on camera flashes to prevent the paintings from fading, and a limit on the number of visitors inside a cave at one time. The entire complex is highly disciplined, featuring informative signboards in multiple languages and requiring tourists to remain silent and remove their footwear before entering sacred spaces. Overall, the trip was a deeply enriching and mesmerising experience that gave us a profound sense of pride in our ancient cultural heritage.

question: Following are the ways to preserve ‘World Heritage Sites.’ Transfer these points into a small paragraph. Suggest a title. Practical conservation of posterity Human/animal

Preservation of World Heritage Sites
World Heritage Sites are the wealth of the people. We must take ownership to ensure that we not only maintain them but also preserve and conserve them for our future generations. For this, human and animal trespassing should be prohibited. They must be considered sacred, and access to them should be monitored. Also, the local administrative bodies responsible for their upkeep must be held accountable for their maintenance.

question: Write a ‘tourism leaflet’ on the following: Your home town Make use of the following points: (i) How to reach there? (ii) Accommodation facilities (iii) What to see and visit?/Attractions of the place
answer:
Visit Mumbai: The city of dreams!

How to reach there?

By Road: Well-connected by highways. Frequent state transport (MSRTC) and private buses run from cities like Pune and Nashik.
By Rail: Superbly connected. Major trains arrive at Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj Terminus (CSMT) and Mumbai Central.
By Air: Flights land at the Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj International Airport.

Accommodation facilities

Budget-friendly hotels and tourist guesthouses near Dadar and CSMT.
Premium 5-star seaside hotels like The Taj Mahal Palace in Colaba.

What to see and visit?/Attractions of the place

Gateway of India & Marine Drive: Iconic landmarks to enjoy the sea breeze and stunning views.
CSMT Building: A magnificent UNESCO World Heritage Site famous for its Gothic architecture.
Juhu Beach: Perfect for watching the sunset and enjoying local street food.
Siddhivinayak Temple: A highly revered and famous spiritual destination.

Places of interest nearby

Elephanta Caves: Famous rock-cut cave temples located just a short ferry ride away.
Sanjay Gandhi National Park: A massive forest reserve inside the city, famous for the ancient Kanheri Caves.

Best time to visit

November to February: The winter months offer pleasant, cool weather that is perfect for open-air sightseeing.

Shopping Attractions

Colaba Causeway & Linking Road: Famous street markets for trendy clothes, junk jewellery, and footwear at low prices.
Crawford Market: Great for buying traditional spices, dry fruits, and gifts.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.3 - World Heritage

question: Write a ‘tourism leaflet’ on the following: A historical place Make use of the following points: (i) How to reach there? (ii) Accommodation facilities
answer:
Visit Raigad Fort: The capital of the Maratha Empire!

How to reach there?

By Road: Raigad is easily accessible by road from major cities. It is about 140 km from Mumbai and 130 km from Pune. Regular State Transport (MSRTC) buses run from Mumbai, Pune, and Mahad directly to the foothills of the fort.
By Rail: The nearest railway station is Mangoan (about 30 km away) on the Konkan Railway route, from where you can take a local taxi or bus.
By Air: The nearest airports are in Mumbai and Pune.

Accommodation facilities:

MTDC Resort: Located right on top of the fort, offering clean cottages and standard rooms.
Local Homestays and Lodges: Available in the base village (Pachad), offering authentic Maharashtrian hospitality and delicious local cuisine.

What to see and visit?/Attractions of the place

The Maha Darwaja: The main, massive entrance of the fort, beautifully flanked by two huge bastions.
Raj Sabha (The Grand Court): The historical site where Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj’s coronation took place. It features a spectacular statue of the great king.
Takmak Tok (Punishment Point): A thrilling 1,200-foot-tall vertical cliff from where convicts were thrown off in ancient times. It offers a panoramic view of the deep valleys.
The Samadhi of Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj: A peaceful and sacred memorial place where the great king was laid to rest.

Places of interest nearby

Gandharpale Caves: Located near Mahad (approx. 25 km away), these are a group of 30 ancient Buddhist rock-cut caves.
Jagadishwar Temple: A beautiful, historic stone temple dedicated to Lord Shiva, located right on the fort complex itself.
Pachad Village: Located at the base of the fort, home to the ruins of the palace of Rajmata Jijau (Shivaji Maharaj’s mother).

Best time to visit

September to March (Winter & Post-Monsoon): This is the ideal time to visit as the weather is cool and pleasant for climbing and exploring the expansive fort.
July to August (Monsoon): Visit during the rainy season to see the fort completely covered in thick mist, lush green carpets, and roaring seasonal waterfalls.

Shopping Attractions

Handcrafted Souvenirs: Beautiful wooden replicas of Raigad Fort and small statues of Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj.
Local Spices & Honey: Organic, pure forest honey collected by local tribes and traditional Konkani spices.
Local Snacks: Freshly made Pithla Bhakri (a local chickpea-flour dish) and local pickles.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.3 - World Heritage

question: Write a ‘tourism leaflet’ on the following: A place of natural beauty Make use of the following points: (i) How to reach there? (ii0 Accommodation facilities
answer:
Explore the Kaas Plateau: The Valley of Flowers of Maharashtra!

How to reach there?

By Road: Kaas Plateau is located about 25 km from Satara city. It is roughly 140 km from Pune and 270 km from Mumbai. State transport (MSRTC) buses and private taxis ply regularly from Satara bus stand to the plateau.
By Rail: The nearest railway station is Satara Railway Station, which is well-connected to Pune, Mumbai, and Kolhapur.
By Air: The nearest airport is Pune International Airport.

Accommodation facilities

MTDC and Eco-Resorts: Located near Kaas Lake and Bamnoli village, offering beautiful lakeside views.
Homestays: Available in the local villages surrounding the plateau, offering a chance to experience authentic rural hospitality and fresh home-cooked Maharashtrian food.
Hotels in Satara: For premium luxury and budget hotels, staying in Satara city (25 km away) is highly convenient.

What to see and visit?/Attractions of the place

The Wildflower Carpet: Witness the plateau change its colour every few weeks! Millions of tiny, rare endemic wild flowers,like the pink Smilacina, purple Karvi, and yellow Mickey Mouse flowers,bloom simultaneously.
Kaas Lake: A serene, crystal-clear perennial lake situated just at the edge of the plateau. It is an ideal spot for a peaceful walk or a relaxing family picnic.
Kumudini Lake: A unique, beautiful pond located nearby that is completely blanketed with floating Kumudini (water lily) flowers.

Places of interest nearby

Thoseghar Waterfalls (20 km away): One of India's highest and cleanest waterfalls, featuring a series of cascading falls amidst a deep, green valley.
Sajjangad Fort (15 km away): A peaceful hill fort that houses the sacred samadhi of Saint Ramdas Swami, offering spiritual tranquility and panoramic mountain views.
Bamnoli Village (15 km away): A scenic coastal-like village on the banks of the Koyna River backwaters, where you can enjoy boating to the dense Vasota Fort jungle.

Best time to visit

Late August to October (The Blooming Season): This is the peak time to visit. Right as the heavy monsoons begin to recede, the flowers burst into full bloom, turning the entire landscape into a colourful paradise.

Shopping Attractions

Kandi Pedha: The world-famous, rich, and delicious traditional sweet of Satara.
Organic Forest Produce: Pure wild honey, organic turmeric, and locally grown finger millet (Nachni).
Handicrafts: Locally woven wooden items and beautiful flower-themed souvenirs.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.3 - World Heritage

question: Write a ‘tourism leaflet’ on the following: A place of pilgrimage Make use of the following points: (i) How to reach there? (ii) Accommodation facilities
answer:
Discover Serenity: A Traveller’s Guide to Divine Varanasi!

How to reach there?

By Air: Lal Bahadur Shastri International Airport (VNS) is located at Babatpur, roughly 24 km from the city center. Regular flights connect Varanasi to Delhi, Mumbai, Bengaluru, and Kolkata.
By Rail: Varanasi Junction (BSB) and Pandit Deen Dayal Upadhyaya Junction (DDU) are the primary railheads, serving premium trains such as the Vande Bharat Express and Rajdhani Express.
By Road: An extensive network of national highways (such as NH-19) connects Varanasi to neighbouring states and major cities like Lucknow, Allahabad (Prayagraj), and Patna.

Accommodation facilities

Heritage Hotels & Havelis: Experience royal hospitality at restored riverside mansions like BrijRama Palace or Taj Nadesar Palace.
Mid-Range Hotels & Guest Houses: Located near the main town area (Godowlia and Cantonment), providing comfortable amenities, Wi-Fi, and room service.
Dharamshalas & Ashrams: For a truly immersive spiritual experience, stay at traditional ashrams near the ghats, offering simple, clean, and highly economical lodging.

What to see and visit?/Attractions of the place

Kashi Vishwanath Temple: The spiritual heart of the city, dedicated to Lord Shiva. Its magnificent golden spires and intense spiritual ambience make it a must-visit.
The Iconic Ghats: There are over 84 ghats lining the Ganges. Dashashwamedh Ghat is the liveliest, Assi Ghat is famous for morning yoga and cultural events, and Manikarnika Ghat offers a sobering look at the eternal rituals of life and death.
Ganga Aarti: A mesmerising evening ritual at Dashashwamedh Ghat. Priests perform a synchronised choreographic prayer with massive brass lamps, accompanied by chants and cymbals.

Places of interest nearby

Sarnath (10 km away): A crucial Buddhist pilgrimage site where Lord Buddha delivered his very first sermon after attaining enlightenment. Don't miss the Dhamek Stupa and the Sarnath Archaeological Museum.
Ramnagar Fort (14 km away): Located on the opposite bank of the Ganges, this 18th-century sandstone fort features a quirky museum displaying vintage cars, royal palanquins, and antique weaponry.
Chunargarh Fort (approx. 40 km away): An ancient structure overlooking the Ganges, steeped in history, myths, and deep-rooted architectural beauty.

Best time to visit

October to March (Winter): This is the peak and most pleasant season. The weather is cool and breezy, making daytime sightseeing incredibly comfortable.
Special Mention (Dev Deepawali): If you visit during Karthik Purnima (usually November), you will witness Dev Deepawali. Millions of earthen lamps (diyas) light up the ghats, creating a breathtaking visual spectacle.

Shopping Attractions

Banarasi Silk Sarees: Universally acclaimed for their fine silk and exquisite gold or silver brocade (Zari) work. Head to the local markets of Chowk or Peeli Kothi to buy directly from weavers.
Spiritual Souvenirs: Pick up Rudraksha beads, brass idols of deities, crystal Shivlings, and wooden toys.
Local Delicacies: Don’t leave without treating your taste buds to the famous Banarasi Paan, Tamatar Chaat, and a refreshing glass of thick, creamy Lassi in a kulhad.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.3 - World Heritage

question: Letter - Write a letter to an expert on the environment. Ask him/her five questions about your concern/doubts about World Heritage Sites. Give him/her three ideas on how to protect them.
answer:
168/7C, Ganesh Niwas,
Thane - 411206
18th May, 2025
Ms. Shweta Chavhan
Environmental activist,
Paryavaran
Thane - 411208
Subject: Concerns and suggestions for protection regarding the World Heritage Site at Ajanta Caves
Respected Madam,
I, Master Suchit Kumar, am a class 10 student of Saraswati High School, Thane. A few days ago, I had the opportunity to visit the famous Ajanta Caves, which are considered part of the World Heritage.
I am greatly disturbed by the state the site is maintained in. The caves are in ruins, and the place is unclean. The lack of security personnel risks the destruction of the historically relevant architecture. The hands of vandals, I also had a few queries as follows:

Could we implement a policy to maintain this site?
Could security be increased to prevent encroachments by animals and human beings?
Could a fee be charged for entry so that the funds may be used for site upkeep?
Can the locals be involved in protecting the site?
Could waste disposal measures be used to ensure cleaner premises?

I also have a few suggestions that we could use to preserve this heritage site:

We could install a CCTV camera to monitor troublemakers.
Placards and signboards could be placed in strategic locations to warn and guide visitors.
Sufficient, well-maintained sanitary facilities could be provided to ensure the place is clean.

I kindly request that you keep us updated on the happenings at the site. My friends and I would be keen to do our part in protecting this very precious Heritage site.
Yours sincerely,
Master Suchit Kumar
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.3 - World Heritage

question: Article - Write an article for a magazine about the World Heritage Sites at risk. Include imaginary interviews with people who are trying to save them. Read your article in front of your class.
answer:
Treasures in Peril: Saving Our World Heritage Sites
UNESCO World Heritage Sites across the globe, from ancient monuments to pristine natural reserves, are currently facing severe threats from climate change, pollution, and war. Iconic landmarks like the Great Barrier Reef are rapidly deteriorating due to rising sea levels, while historical cities suffer from uncontrolled mass tourism. “We are racing against time to restore these sites before they are lost forever,” warns Dr. Elena Vance, a dedicated conservationist fighting to protect fragile ecosystems. Around the world, passionate activists are working tirelessly to fund restorations and implement stricter protection laws, but they constantly face hurdles due to insufficient funding. As youth, we can make a massive difference by spreading awareness and advocating for eco-friendly tourism practices. Ultimately, protecting these global treasures is our collective duty to ensure that our shared history and vibrant planet do not vanish for future generations.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.3 - World Heritage

question: Match the lines with the Figures of Speech. Lines 1. In wondrous merry mood. 2. They were so queer, so very queer. 3. And saw him peep within. 4. The grin grew broad. 5. And shot from ear to ear.
answer:
Lines
Figures of Speech
Explanation

1. In wondrous merry mood.
Transferred Epithet
The adjective “merry” describes the person’s state of mind, but it is transferred to the word “mood”.

2. They were so queer, so very queer.
Repetition
The words “so queer” are repeated for emphasis.

3. And saw him peep within.
Alliteration
The sound of the letter ‘w’ is repeated in “without” and “within” (implied context from Oliver Wendell Holmes’ poem The Height of the Ridiculous).

4. The grin grew broad.
Alliteration/Tautology
The ‘g’ sound is repeated in “grin grew”. (Note: In the context of this specific poem’s textbook exercises, this is often paired with Alliteration or tautology, depending on the specific answer key, but phonetically, it is alliteration.)

5. And shot from ear to ear.
Hyperbole
An exaggeration; a grin cannot literally shoot physically from one ear to the other.

6. He broke into a roar.
Onomatopoeia
The word “roar” imitates the actual sound being made.

7. Ten days and nights with sleepless eye.
Transferred Epithet/Hyperbole
“Sleepless” is transferred from the person to their “eye”. It is also a Hyperbole because staying awake for ten straight days and nights is a massive exaggeration.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.4 - The Height of the Ridiculous

question: Form goups in your class and together compose a short humorous poem. Use jokes, experiences, etc. and convert it to a poetic form.
answer:
The Great Classroom Mystery
We walked into the classroom, ready for the day,
But realized with a horror, we forgot to text and say:
“Hey friend, did you complete the homework due by nine?”
My group members just stared at me, a shiver down their spine!
The teacher walked in smiling, with chalk upon her hand,
We started plotting fake excuses, the best in all the land.
“My dog ate up my notebook!” or “My internet went dead!”
But honestly, the truth was, we just stayed too long in bed.
Then came the sudden pop-quiz, the ultimate betrayal,
Our faces turned as white as ghosts, our confidence grew frail.
I looked across at Rahul, who was staring at the wall,
Hoping the answers would appear, or the ceiling just would fall!
We survived the class together, though our brains were turned to mush,
When the final bell rang loudly, you should have seen the rush!
We might not be the smartest, and our homework might be late,
But as a group of chaotic friends, we think we're doing great!
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.4 - The Height of the Ridiculous

question: Read the following poem and write an appreciation of it with the help of the given points in paragraph format. The Height of the Ridiculous I wrote some lines once on a time In wondrous merry mood
answer:
1Show SolutionThe poem The Height of the Ridiculous’ by Oliver Wendell Holmes is a humorous poem written for enjoyment. with plenty of funny expressions. The main purpose of the poet is to simply entertain his audience. The rhyme scheme of the poem is ‘abcb’. There are many figures of speech, like Hyperbole, Tautology,
Onomatopoeia, Alliteration, etc., but the one that stands out is the transferred epithet. In the line Ten days and nights, with sleepless eye the adjective sleepless should be for the man and not for the eye. This is a humorous poem. The special feature of the poem is the use of expressions that convey funny moments. The reader forms a mental picture and enjoys the poem. My favourite line is The sixth, he burst five buttons. I like this line because I can imagine the way the man must have been laughing till he burst the buttons on his coat. I like the poem because it is light, simple, and entertaining.
subject: English

question: Write a book review on any book of your choice with the help of the following points. Story line Social/Historical content Writing style My thought
answer:
The Room on the Roof by Ruskin Bond

Story line: The story in this book is about an orphaned boy named Rusty. He is a sad and lonely boy, living with his guardian, Mr. Harrison. Rusty, in his search for an identity, dares to venture out of the house and make new friends. He is finally left homeless by Harrison, and this is when he realises what it means to be an orphan in the truest sense. Rusty’s friend comes. to his rescue when he lands Rusty a job teaching English. Things are fine for a while, after which a strange sequence of events occurs, and Rusty ends up homeless again. This time, he is on the verge of leaving for England when his student, Kishen, stops him, and together they decide to start afresh in life on a new note.
Social/Historical content: The story about the Anglo-Indian teenager Rusty bears a striking resemblance to the life of its writer, Ruskin Bond. Despite Ruskin's pain at his parents’ separation and a lonely childhood, he developed an optimistic outlook on life much like Rusty does in the story. It is this teenager’s search for freedom, identity, and friends that creates the story's context and plot. The book was published in 1956, just after India acquired independence. It reflects on a time when a few English remained back in India for good. It depicts how life comes a complete circle for Rusty, from being lonely to being surrounded by friends and yet being lonely again. Only this time around, Rusty is wise enough to realise that he has the potential to begin life afresh.
Writing style: Ruskin Bond has a distinctly poetic writing style. His choice of words and placement of imagery provide for an interesting yet lucid read. Particularly fascinating is the narrative. When the boy finds himself torn between living in India and England. Ruskin is very descriptive and hence uses many adjectives to support his writing, which makes his works delightful to read. He has also cleverly conveyed in the novel how strangers can become part and parcel of Orie’s life by being kind and warm in their interactions. Rusty, the central character in the novel, uses this same idea to make friends for life.
My thought: I love this book because of its simple style and the picture it paints of a teenage boy I can relate to, since we are of the same generation. I also like the multiple emotions the author depicts beautifully in this piece. I would like to read other works of Ruskin. Bond, because of his expertise in making simple things beautiful.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.5 - The Old Man and The Sea: Book Review

question: Classroom Library: Ask all your classmates to donate a book each to the class. In this way everyone will have a book of his/her own to read. Read the book in a week or two.
answer:
To help your class launch this successfully and keep track of all the books, here is a step-by-step game plan you can share with your classmates or teacher.
Step-by-Step Launch Plan:

The Book Drive: Have everyone bring in one book they are willing to share for the school year. Label the inside cover with the owner’s name so it doesn’t get lost.
Build the Catalogue: List all the books on a sheet of paper or a simple spreadsheet. This is your “library catalogue,” so everyone knows what choices are available.
First Reading Round: Everyone picks a book to start with. Spend the next two weeks reading your chosen book during free periods or at home.
The Fortnightly Review: Hold your first presentation day! Share a quick thought about the book you just finished before handing it off to the next person.
The Great Exchange: Pass your finished book to a classmate who wants to read it next, check a new one off the catalogue, and repeat the cycle!
subject: English

question: How much did Della save for Christmas? What does it show?
answer: Della saved only $ 1.87 for Christmas. This shows how poor they were and how much they loved each other, because even with so little, she wanted to get Jim a special gift.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Why did Della take pride in the beauty of her hair?
answer: Della took pride in her hair because it was her most prized possession. It was like a crown for her, and she felt beautiful with it.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Why and how did Della sell her hair? Do you think she did well?
answer: Della sells her hair to Madame Sofronie for twenty dollars, which is just enough money to buy a platinum fob chain that costs twenty-one dollars. Della made a mistake by selling her hair because Jim had brought her a comb. Which becomes useless since she already cut off her hair.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Why did Jim sell his gold watch? Do you think he did well?
answer: Jim sold his gold watch, which belonged to his father and grandfather, to buy a set of beautiful combs that Della yearned for. Jim made a mistake by selling his watch to buy the combs because Della had already sold her hair to buy him a platinum chain for his gold watch.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Why did Jim and Della want to present the gifts?
answer: Jim and Della wanted to present the gifts to show their love and affection for each other. They wanted to make each other happy on Christmas.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Why did Della feel sad on losing her hair?
answer: Della felt sad because she loved her hair. It was a part of her beauty, and she felt less confident without it. She also felt that her husband might not love her the same without her beautiful hair.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: What did Jim bring out from his coat? Describe the scene.
answer: Jim brought out a small, beautifully wrapped package from his coat. Della was excited and curious. She opened it and found the combs she had always wanted, but then she burst into tears because she had no hair to wear them with. Jim was shocked and confused at first.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: How beautiful was the gold watch chain? Would you have liked to own it?
answer: The gold watch chain was described as perfect, and Jim was really excited about it. It sounds really beautiful, and I would love to own something so shiny and fancy.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: How did the Magi want to celebrate their Christmas (OR) Who were the first people to give Christmas gifts?
answer: The Magi were the first people to give Christmas gifts. They gave gifts to baby Jesus.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: What is the moral of the story?
answer: The moral of the story is that the true meaning of Christmas and gift-giving is love and sacrifice. It’s about giving without expecting anything in return. Jim and Della showed the ultimate act of love by sacrificing their most prized possessions for each other.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Complete: Use the chart below to explain the situational irony in ‘The Gift of the Magi’. For each character, identify what is expected to happen and what actually happens.
answer:
SITUATIONAL IRONY

What Della plans: To give Jim a wonderful fob chain for Christmas.
What actually happens: Jim cannot use Della’s gift of a fob chain for his watch because he had already sold his watch to get her a Christmas gift.

What Jim plans: To give Della a wonderful Christmas gift in the form of a set of tortoise shell combs.

What actually happens: Della cannot use Jim’s gift of combs because she has cut and sold her hair to get him a Christmas gift.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Think and answer on your own in a few sentences, in your notebook. What would you do if you ran short of money, to buy a birthday gift for one of your parents/siblings?
answer: If I ran short of money to buy a gift for one of my parents/siblings, I would borrow from someone else close to me. For example, if I wanted to buy a bag for my sister and didn't have enough money, I would ask my mother to lend it to me. If I wanted to buy something for Mom and did not have the money, I would borrow it from Dad.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Think and answer on your own in a few sentences, in your notebook. Think of two alternatives that could have prevented Della as well as Jim from selling their most precious possessions.
answer: Both knew that they were poor and couldn’t afford expensive gifts. So they could have just given each other small token gifts of flowers or cards. This would have prevented them from selling their most precious possessions.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Think and answer on your own in a few sentences, in your notebook. Justify the title of the story, ‘The Gift of the Magi’.
answer: The Magi were three wise men who brought gifts for baby Jesus during Christmas. They knew how to give and gave unique gifts with special emphasis. In the same way. Della and Jim gave each other unique gifts. The author compares their giving to the gifts the Magi gave to baby Jesus. He calls them the Magi because of their wisdom in realising that unselfish love is the greatest of all gifts.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Think and answer on your own in a few sentences, in your notebook. Gifts that you buy have fixed prices/rates. How can the buyer increase the value of a gift bought for someone very dear in the family
answer: The buyer can increase the value of a gift bought for someone very dear in the family by wrapping it with words of love and gratitude. He/She can also write something loving on a card and attach it to the gift.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Think and answer on your own in a few sentences, in your notebook. What situation is the climax of the story? How does it end up as an Anti-climax?
answer: The climax should have been the happiness and joy on the faces of Jim and Della when they see their gifts. It ends up as an anti-climax because though they had got what they coveted, neither of them could now use the gifts.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Think and answer on your own in a few sentences, in your notebook. If you share this story with some friends in a junior class, how would they react in the end?

subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Think and answer on your own in a few sentences, in your notebook. Who were the very first people to give Christmas gifts? What had inspired them to do so?

subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 4.6 - The Gift of the Magi

question: Think and answer on your own in a few sentences, in your notebook. At the end of the story, O Henry remarks that of all who give gifts, Della and Jim were the wisest.
answer: Jim and Della were initially foolish enough to believe they needed to give material gifts to show their love for one another and that they had to sacrifice their most precious possessions to do so. However, they were also wise enough to know later that unselfish love is the greatest of all gifts.
